coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous labeling and multicolor fluorescence imaging of multiple immune cells on liver frozen section by polychromatic quantum dots below freezing points
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yan-Fei
Cheng, Kai
Zhong, Zi-Tao
Hou, Xiao-Lin
An, Chang-Zhi
Chen, Wei
Liu, Bo
Zhao, Yuan-Di
coredata.dc:description: A method for simultaneous labeling and multicolor fluorescence imaging of different hepatic immune cells below freezing point is established based on quantum dots. In the experiment, carbon quantum dots with emission wavelength of 435 nm, CdTe@CdS quantum dots at 542 nm and CdSe@ZnS quantum dots at 604 nm are synthesized respectively, it is found that when the mass fractions of KCl (as antifreeze) are 12 %, 14 %, and 12 %, respectively, the three quantum dot dispersion systems remain liquid state at −20 °C. After they are conjugated with the corresponding secondary antibodies, agarose gel electrophoresis, circular dichroism and capillary electrophoresis confirm the effectiveness of conjugation. By indirect immunofluorescence method, the above three quantum dot fluorescent probes are used to simultaneously and specifically target a variety of liver immune cells, and the multi-color simultaneous imaging of different liver immune cells is realized under the same excitation wavelength, it is found that hepatic macrophages are arranged radially in the liver, hepatic stellate cells present punctate distribution, and hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cells present circular distribution, which is consistent with the results of H&E staining and ultrathin section TEM. This study provides an important technical means for elucidating the structure and function of the liver.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor quantum dots
Freezing point
Liver frozen section
Simultaneous multicolor imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of QD fluorescent probes (Qds-Ab) based on BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs
Capillary electrophoresis characterization of QDs-Ab fluorescent probes
Selection of low temperature environment for cells
Fluorescence imaging of liver frozen section
Research on low-freezing point quantum dot dispersion system
TEM of mouse liver ultrathin section

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence and bright-field images of BCDs (A, B), GQDs (C, D), RQDs (E, F) dispersion systems containing KCl of different concentrations at −20 °C; fluorescence spectra of BCDs (a) containing 12 % KCl, GQDs (b) containing 14 % KCl and RQDs (c) containing 12 % KCl at 37 °C (G) and −20 °C (H), and BCDs (a’), GQDs (b’) and RQDs (b’) dispersions without salt.
Fluorescence lifetime of BCDs (A), GQDs (B), RQDs (C) before (a) and after adding 12%, 14% and 12% KCl (b).

Absorption (a) and fluorescence (b) spectra of BCDs (A), GQDs (B), RQDs (C); and absorption (a’) and fluorescence (b’) spectra of BCDs-KAb (A), GQDs-HAb (B), RQDs-Lab (C); agarose gel electrophoresis (D-F), zeta (G-I) and capillary electrophoresis (J-L) of three fluorescent probes.
Zeta potential and hydrated particle size (A-C), PDI (D-F), photobleaching resistance (G-I), and circular dichroism (J-L) of BCDs-KAb, GQDs-HAb, RQDs-LAb.
H&E stained section of mouse liver; CV: central vein, HC: hepatic parenchymal cell, LSEC: liver sinusoidal endothelial cell, KC: kupffer cell.
TEM images of different regions of liver ultrathin section; LSEC: liver sinusoidal endothelial cells, KC: kupffer cell, HSC: hepatic stellate cell.
Bright-field, fluorescence imaging and fluorescence spectra of macrophage on liver sections labeled by BCDs-KAb at −20 and 37 °C, and AMCA-Ab at 37 °C.
Bright-field, fluorescence imaging and fluorescence spectra of HSCs on liver sections labeled by GQDs-HAb at −20 and 37 °C, and FITC-Ab at 37 °C.
Bright-field, fluorescence imaging and fluorescence spectra of LSECs on liver sections labeled by RQD-LAb at −20 and 37 °C, and Cy3-Ab at 37 °C.
Fluorescence imaging and fluorescence spectra of KCs, HSCs and LSECs labeled by fluorescent dye-labeled secondary antibody (FL-2nd-Ab) at 37 °C.
Fluorescence imaging and fluorescence spectra of KCs, HSCs and LSECs labeled by QDs-Ab on liver section at 37 °C.
Fluorescence imaging and fluorescence spectra of KCs, HSCs and LSECs labeled by QDs-Ab on liver section at −20 °C.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous labeling and multicolor fluorescence imaging of multiple immune cells on liver frozen section by polychromatic quantum dots below freezing points

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Britton Chance Center for Biomedical Photonics at Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics - Hubei Bioinformatics & Molecular Imaging Ke Laboratory
College of Life Science and Technology
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A method for simultaneous labeling and multicolor fluorescence imaging of different hepatic immune cells below freezing point is established based on quantum dots. In the experiment, carbon quantum dots with emission wavelength of 435 nm, CdTe@CdS quantum dots at 542 nm and CdSe@ZnS quantum dots at 604 nm are synthesized respectively, it is found that when the mass fractions of KCl (as antifreeze) are 12 %, 14 %, and 12 %, respectively, the three quantum dot dispersion systems remain liquid state at −20 °C. After they are conjugated with the corresponding secondary antibodies, agarose gel electrophoresis, circular dichroism and capillary electrophoresis confirm the effectiveness of conjugation. By indirect immunofluorescence method, the above three quantum dot fluorescent probes are used to simultaneously and specifically target a variety of liver immune cells, and the multi-color simultaneous imaging of different liver immune cells is realized under the same excitation wavelength, it is found that hepatic macrophages are arranged radially in the liver, hepatic stellate cells present punctate distribution, and hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cells present circular distribution, which is consistent with the results of H&E staining and ultrathin section TEM. This study provides an important technical means for elucidating the structure and function of the liver.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: Liver fibrosis, liver cancer, fatty liver and other refractory liver diseases [1–3] have seriously threatened human health, which is closely related to the special anatomical structure and immune cells’ composition of the liver. Although researchers have known the liver's anatomical structure and cellular composition for hundreds of years, it is still difficult to understand the fine three-dimensional anatomical structure of the complete liver [4], especially its intricate vasculature, which makes it hard to accurately locate immune cells [5–6]. Therefore, an in-depth understanding of the liver structure and the distribution of immune cells is of great significance for us to better understand the functions of the liver in immunity, digestion, drug metabolism and secretion, and the mechanism of liver related diseases [7].

Using antigen–antibody reaction to label liver immune cells is a cell localization technique with strong specificity and high sensitivity, since there are many types of immune cells in the liver, in order to understand their different distributions in the liver simultaneously, the labeling technology must also have the ability to simultaneously target and identify multiple types of liver cells. Among the cell labeling techniques, common methods include immunohistochemistry [8], immunofluorescence [9], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [10]. Immunofluorescence technology [11] is based on the specific binding between antigens and antibodies, and then using the fluorescence of materials to locate or qualitatively analyze the antigens in cells or tissues. Compared with immunohistochemistry and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, it has the advantages of simple operation and high sensitivity, it can realize fluorescence labeling imaging by fluorescence microscope, so it is especially suitable for localization research of low content targets [12]. Immunofluorescence includes direct immunofluorescence [13] and indirect immunofluorescence [14], the former has simple steps, but its reagents are expensive, and one antibody can only recognize one antigen, while the latter needs low reagent cost, and the use of secondary antibodies can also amplify the fluorescent signal to improve sensitivity, and one fluorescent secondary antibody can detect multiple antigens or antibodies [15], so it can be well applied to the simultaneous labeling and imaging of different liver immune cells.

Section observation is a common method for cell analysis at the tissue level, generally, including paraffin section [16], ultrathin section [17], vibration section [18], etc., these tissue section methods are often cumbersome to operate, and antigen activity is destroyed to a certain extent after a series of treatments, and the organizations have a strong spontaneous fluorescence and nonspecific fluorescence [19]. Frozen sections [20] have attracted much attention due to the advantages of high antigen immunity, simple operation, less autofluorescence, and complete RNA information, in particular, it avoids the inactivation of proteins and enzymes and maintains the activity of cell surface antigens [21]. Therefore, simultaneous multicolor imaging of different hepatic immune cells on liver frozen section by immunofluorescence technique can obtain their visual distribution in the liver.

At present, the fluorescent markers used for cell or tissue fluorescence imaging are mostly organic fluorescent dyes, such as common cyanine dyes, thiazine/oxazine dyes, etc. [22–24]. However, such fluorescent substances also have many obvious disadvantages, such as the narrow excitation wavelength range, which usually requires multiple wavelengths to excite different dyes to emit different fluorescence, then it is difficult to image simultaneously; the fluorescence emission peaks are severely tailed and easy to overlap each other; poor photostability leads to quick quenching of fluorescence. These all directly affect their application in biological imaging.

Quantum dot (QD), as an excellent nano-fluorescent material for labeling and imaging, has a broad and continuous excitation spectrum [25] compared with traditional organic fluorescent dye, and the multicolor fluorescence emission of QDs with different particle sizes can be excited by the same light source, realizing simultaneous labeling of multiple signal sources; the emission spectrum is narrow and symmetrical [26], which avoids the overlap between fluorescence spectra, and reduces the mutual interference; its quantum yield is high [27], and the fluorescence intensity can reach about 20 times that of rhodamine 6G; it has strong photobleaching resistance [28] and can realize long-term observation for target substance, which provides a powerful tool for long-term monitoring of biomolecular interaction in cell; and its modification is easy [29], different functional groups can be modified on the surface of QD, and it can also be coupled with antibodies, nucleic acids and small molecules; the fluorescence lifetime is long [30], reaching tens of nanoseconds, which can effectively reduce the interference of biological samples’ autofluorescence. Therefore, if multiple fluorescence QDs are linked with antibodies against different liver cell receptors, it could achieve simultaneous labeling of multiple liver cells. Therefore, as an ideal fluorescence marker, QD can play an important role in simultaneous labeling imaging of different liver immune cells.

Taking advantage of the excellent fluorescence performance of QDs, a new technology for rapid labeling of multiple immune cells at −20 °C to visualize their spatial distribution on liver slice was established by immunofluorescence. The results showed that the fluorescence performance of QDs is significantly improved at low temperature. After coupling different antibodies, the hepatic macrophages, hepatic stellate cells, and hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cells on mouse liver frozen section can be synchronously labeled and imaged below freezing point.

1‑ethyl - (3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC HCl, 98 %) was purchased from Aladdin reagent (Shanghai) Co., ltd, N-hydroxythiosuccinimide (sulfo-NHS) was purchased from Beijing Soleibo Technology Co., ltd.; morpholine ethanesulfonic acid buffer (MES, 0.05 mol/L, pH 5.5), borax buffer (Na2B4O7, 0.05 mol/L, pH 9.3) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., ltd.; 4 % paraformaldehyde, Triton X-100 (TX-100), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Wuhan Jieyangsheng Technology Co., ltd.; potassium chloride and 2-mercaptoethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. anti-fluorescence quenching mountant, chicken anti-mouse GFAP (anti-GFAP), rabbit anti-mouse EMR1 (anti-EMR1) were purchased from Wuhan Sevier Biotechnology Co., ltd.; goat anti-mouse CD31 (anti-CD31), donkey anti-rabbit IgG (KAb), donkey anti-chicken IgY (HAb), donkey anti-goat IgG (LAb) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., ltd.; AMCA-labeled KAb, FITC-labeled HAb, Cy3-labeled LAb were purchased from Sangon Bioengineering (Shanghai) Co., ltd.; UFC505096 ultrafiltration centrifuge tubes were purchased from Millipore (USA), other reagents were analytically pure products, and experimental water was deionized water.

The instruments used in the experiment include HT7700 biological transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan), Nano-ZS90 nanometer particle sizer (Malvern, UK), QE6500 fluorescence spectrometer (Ocean Optics, USA), LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA), UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), DLSB5-40 °C cryogenic coolant circulation pump (Henan Jinbo Instrument Manufacturing Co., ltd., China), J-810 circular dichromatograph CD (Jasco, Japan), FV3000 confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan).

During the simultaneous labeling and imaging of multiple signal sources, the overlapping emission spectra will interfere with the identification of different antigens, while the blue, green and red colors have obvious discrimination, and their emission wavelengths have large difference, which could effectively avoid the trouble caused by the overlapping. Since blue fluorescence cannot be well detected by the human eyes, blue QDs should be used to label the antigen with the largest content in multiple labeling experiment. Compared with red fluorescence, the human eyes were more sensitive to green fluorescence, so green QDs were more suitable for labeling antigen with relatively minimal content.

Among the hepatic immune cells, the proportion of hepatic stellate cells was at least 10 %, and that of hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cells was 60 %, which was more than that of hepatic macrophages (30 %), but hepatic macrophages were densely distributed and easier to identify. Therefore, among the three immunofluorescence markers, blue QDs were selected to label hepatic macrophages, green QDs to label hepatic stellate cells, and red QDs to label hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cells.

The synthesis of QDs with different fluorescence emissions was based on the previous published papers. Since only carbon QDs could emit blue fluorescence, and blue carbon QDs (BCDs) had the highest quantum yield and the best fluorescence stability, so the BCDs were used as the blue fluorescent labeling material. Similarly, among multicolor CdTe/CdS QDs and multicolor CdSe/ZnS QDs, green CdTe/CdS QDs (GQDs) and red CdSe/ZnS QDs (RQDs) had the best quantum yield and fluorescence stability, so GQDs and RQDs were used as green and red fluorescent markers, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of QD fluorescent probes (Qds-Ab) based on BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The combination of QDs and antibodies could be divided into two steps: activation and coupling, the activation of carboxyl group had the best effect in a weakly acidic environment, while the coupling process was better in weakly alkaline environment. Firstly, EDC and sulfo-NHS were equilibrated to room temperature, and BCDs, GQDs and RQDs were dissolved in MES solution (pH 5.5) in the ratio of QDs: EDC: sulfo-NHS = 1:10:30, respectively, and reacted at room temperature for 15 min, then 1.4 μL of 2‑mertoethyl alcohol was added to quench the reaction; then, the pH of the reaction system was adjusted to 7.2 ∼ 7.4 with 1 M NaOH solution, KAb, HAb, LAb were added to the BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs reaction systems respectively, and reacted at room temperature in the dark for 2 h, the free unreacted substances were removed by ultrafiltration centrifuge tubes; finally, BCDs-KAb、GQDs-HAb、RQDs-LAb fluorescent probes were obtained respectively, and stored at 4 °C in the dark for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capillary electrophoresis characterization of QDs-Ab fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: Capillary electrophoresis (CE) was performed by a CE device built by the laboratory. A quartz capillary with a length of 60 cm (inner diameter: 75 µm) was intercepted, and the protective layer on the surface of the capillary was removed by burning at a distance of 35 cm from the injection port and used as a fluorescence detection window. At the same time, the capillary was fixed on the detection stage of an inverted fluorescence microscope with adhesive tape, and the detection window was directly above the objective lens. The capillary tube was washed with 1 M NaOH, 1 M HCl, and electrophoresis buffer (Na2B4O7, 25 mM, pH 9.3) for 20 min before injection, and the system was preelectrophoreted with electrophoresis buffer at high voltage for 10 min to reach an equilibrium state. The samples were injected from positive electrode for 10 s, and the fluorescence spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrometer connected to an inverted fluorescence microscope. To ensure the accuracy of the experiment, the capillary tube was also washed with 1 M NaOH, 1 M HCl, and electrophoresis buffer for 20 min before the next electrophoresis.

In order to investigate the stability of fluorescent probes, the zeta potential, particle size and polydispersity index (PDI) of QDs-Ab and QDs were measured after storage for 0、5、10、15、20、30 d, respectively. The fluorescence stability experiments of the probes were performed by placing the QDs-Ab and rhodamine 6G under a benchtop UV lamp, and irradiated with 365 nm excitation light for 60 min, the fluorescence spectra were measured at 0、5、10、20、30、40、50、60 min respectively, the photobleaching resistance of the probes was investigated by the change of fluorescence intensity, three parallel samples were set for each group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of low temperature environment for cells
ce:section.ce:para: The BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs dispersion systems containing different mass fractions (5 %, 10 %, 11 %, 12 %, 13 %, 14 %, 15 % and 20 %) of KCl were prepared respectively, and placed in a pre-cooled cryogenic circulation pump (-20 °C) and maintained the temperature overnight. These QDs dispersion systems were observed next day, and photos were taken under sunlight and 365 nm excitation light respectively, and the fluorescence spectra of BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs dispersion systems were collected at −20 °C and 37 °C.

The H&E staining of the mouse liver included fixation, dehydration, transparency, embedding, sectioning, dewaxing, to water, staining, dehydration, transparency, and blocking, respectively. Firstly, fresh mouse liver tissue block (thickness less than 0.5 cm) was fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde, dehydrated in alcohol with different concentrations, and made transparent with xylene, and then embedded in paraffin. The obtained tissue block sections (thickness of 5 µm) were flattened in hot water and pasted on anti-detachment glass slides, which were dried in a 45 °C oven. Before staining, the sections were dewaxed to water by xylene and absolute ethanol, respectively. The nuclei was stained in hematoxylin for 5 min, the cytoplasm was stained in eosin for 2 min, the stained sections were dehydrated by alcohol again, the resin was added to seal the slides after xylene was transparent. All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Experiment Ethics Committee of Huazhong University of Science and Technology (IACUC Number: S904).

The liver tissue was cut into small pieces less than 1 mm3, fixed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for more than 2 h, and washed 3 times with PBS for 15 min each; then fixed with 1 % osmic acid for 2 h, washed 3 times with PBS for 15 min each time; gradient dehydration in different concentrations (30 %、50 %、70 %、90 %、95 % and 100 %) ethanol respectively, then infiltrated and embedded through epoxy resin; they were cured in the oven at different temperatures (37, 45 and 60 °C) for 12, 12 and 48 h respectively, the liver tissue was sectioned by an ultramicrotome with a thickness of 50 nm, stained with 2 % uranyl acetate for 10 min, and stained with lead citrate for 10 min, and finally observed by transmission electron microscope.

Firstly, the frozen microtome was pre-cooled to −20 °C in advance, and then the mouse was anesthetized in the abdominal cavity, fresh liver tissue was extracted and gently shaken in PBS to remove residual blood on its surface. The smallest liver tissue block was selected (too large of the tissue block would lead to section edge curling), the PBS on the surface was sucked dry, and the sample was placed on the pre-cooled sample tray, and the sample surface was coated with a layer of frozen section embedding agent (OCT). When the liver tissue block was solidified on a freezing microtome, the OCT was removed by extensive repair, when the liver tissue was exposed, the liver frozen sections with a thickness of 10 µm were cut and dipped on detachment-proof glass slides, with 2 slices for each set of slide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of liver frozen section
ce:section.ce:para: The liver frozen sections were dried at room temperature for 15 min, and the sections to be stained were circled with an immunohistochemical pen so that the antibody could be incubated effectively, and then soaked in PBS for 10 min to remove the embedding medium on the surface. The immunofluorescence steps of liver frozen sections were fixation, permeability, blocking, first antibody incubation, fluorescent second antibody incubation, and sealing piece. Firstly, the samples were fixed in ice acetone pre-cooled at 4 °C for 10 min, and washed with PBS 3 times, 5 min each; and then permeated with 0.3 % TX-100 for 30 min and washed with PBS for 3 times, 5 min each time. The samples were blocked by 3 % BSA for 1 h (incubated in a wet box to prevent the sections from dry), and the primary antibody was directly added without washing, and the fluorescent secondary antibody was added for further incubation after washing with PBS, and the excess unreacted fluorescent substances were removed by PBS. anti-fluorescence quenching mounting medium was added dropwise, and the coverslips were mounted and stored at 4 °C in the dark, the imaging effect of liver frozen section was observed with laser confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on low-freezing point quantum dot dispersion system
ce:section.ce:para: When the cells were in a frozen environment without any protective agent, the formation of ice crystal inside or outside the cells will cause the cells to shrink, affecting the tissue morphology, and finally leading to poor staining of frozen section. Since the fluorescent labeling and imaging of liver frozen sections will be carried out at −20 °C, therefore, the addition of antifreeze can effectively reduce the solution freezing point in the whole process, thus avoiding irreversible damage to cells.

The previous research showed that KCl was a kind of antifreeze with good performance, therefore, in this paper, BCDs, GQDs and RQDs dispersion systems containing different mass fractions of KCl were prepared respectively, and the solutions at −20 °C were investigated. The results showed when the mass fractions of KCl were 12 %, 14 % and 12 % (Fig. 1 A ∼ F), the dispersion systems of BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs did not solidify and still remained in liquid state, and they could still emit bright blue, green and red fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, respectively.

Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra of BCDs containing 12 % KCl, GQDs containing 14 % KCl, and RQDs containing 12 % KCl were collected at 37 °C and −20 °C, respectively, the fluorescence spectra of three dispersions were simultaneously collected at 37 °C. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of three dispersion systems decreased to some extent after adding KCl (Fig. 1G); however, their fluorescence intensity at −20 °C (Fig. 1H, a-c) were improved in varying degrees compared with those at 37 °C (Fig. 1G, a-c), and even higher than those of KCl-free dispersing systems at 37 °C (Fig. 1G, a’- c’). The above results showed that the fluorescence performance of QDs at −20 °C was significantly improved after adding KCl as antifreeze. Therefore, during the subsequent liver frozen section imaging, 12 %, 14 % and 12 % KCl were added to the culture medium containing BCDs, GQDs and RQDs, respectively.

The fluorescence of quantum dot was partially quenched when KCl was added. The main reason was that metal ion adsorbed on the surface of the quantum dot, captured the electron in the guide band of the quantum dot, and then changed the surface state energy level, so as to increase the non-radiative recombination of electron-hole, which led to the fluorescence quenching of the quantum dot [31].

At the same time, the fluorescence lifetimes of the three quantum dots after adding KCl in the quantum dot solution were tested. The results showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of the three quantum dots were 13.2, 19.8 and 32.4 ns, respectively, while 11.6, 15.7 and 26.1 ns after adding salt (Fig. 2 ). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of quantum dots conformed to Stern-Volmer quenching equation, that was, dynamic quenching.
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
t 0
. C
q

F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity before and after the quenching agent was added, kq was the rate constant of dynamic quenching, t and t0 were the fluorescence lifetime in the presence and absence of quench agent, and Cq was the quench agent concentration in solution. The typical feature of dynamic quenching was that decreasing temperature could weaken the quenching effect [32]. This was because the solution viscosity increased with the decrease of temperature, and the movement of molecules slowed down, which would reduce the diffusion coefficient of molecules, thus reducing the quenching constant of bimolecules, and thus weakening the quenching effect. Therefore, when the solution temperature was reduced to −20 °C, the fluorescence intensity of the quantum dot increased in the presence of salt.

In immunofluorescence triple labeling, cross-reaction usually leads to non-specific binding, which affects the specific target recognition, therefore, primary antibodies from different species and corresponding secondary antibodies should be selected. BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs were conjugated with donkey anti-rabbit IgG, donkey anti-chicken IgY, and donkey anti-goat IgG, respectively, and the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were analyzed before and after conjugation. It was found that the first exciton absorption peaks of BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs were located near 329, 495, and 573 nm (Fig. 3A-C, a), respectively, and the fluorescence emission peaks were located at 435, 542, and 604 nm (Fig. 3A-C, b), respectively, the three kinds of fluorescence were spaced relatively widely to avoid spectral overlap.

The first exciton absorption peaks of the conjugated BCDs-KAb, GQDs-HAb, and RQDs-LAb fluorescent probes had little change (Fig. 3A-C, a’), but the fluorescence emission peaks all showed red-shift (Fig. 3A-C, b’), which was caused by the increase of particle size after the QDs coupling with Ab. It was conjectured the size increased after quantum dot was conjugated with antibody, its effective band gap would narrow, and thus its energy decreased, and finally resulted in the red shift of the fluorescence emission spectrum [33]. Besides, after quantum dot was covalently coupled with antibody, one antibody might bind multiple quantum dots, thus reducing the distance between the quantum dots, resulting in enhanced dipole interaction between the quantum dots, so the fluorescence emission peak of the QD-Ab conjugate was red shifted [34]. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of three QDs-Ab was improved, because Ab filled the surface defects of the QDs and inhibited the non-radiative transition, and more energy was released in the form of light, so the fluorescence intensity was enhanced after coupling.

To verify the effectiveness of conjugation, the QDs and QDs-Ab were analyzed by agarose gel electrophoresis. It was found that the bands of BCDs-KAb, GQDs-HAb, RQDs-LAb were delayed compared with those of three QDs (Fig. 3 D-F), due to the increase in the molar mass of QDs after the coupling with Ab. When the conjugates passed through the network-like agarose gel, the resistance to them increased during electrophoresis, which made the movement of conjugates slow down, so there was a tandem difference between the bands of QDs and conjugates, indicating that the Abs were indeed effectively coupled to the QDs, respectively.

Zeta potential analysis showed that the potentials of BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs were −40.9 ± 1.32 mV, −43.7 ± 2.26 mV, −44.6 ± 2.13 mV, respectively (Fig. 3G-I), those of BCDs-KAb, GQDs-HAb, and RQDs-LAb all decreased after conjugation with Ab, which were −30.2 ± 2.0 mV, −37.4 ± 1.25 mV, −36.1 ± 1.36 mV, respectively. This was because the surface of QDs contained abundant carboxyl groups, and the surface negative charge decreased after coupling with Ab, therefore, the zeta potentials were reduced, which further indicated that the QDs were indeed coupled to the Ab protein secondary antibodies.

Capillary electrophoresis analysis showed that the migration times of BCDs, GQDs, and RQDs were 535, 441, and 501 s, respectively (Fig. 3J-L), those of BCDs-KAb, GQDs-HAb, and RQDs-LAb were 385, 289, and 324 s, respectively. The migration times of QDs-Ab conjugates were shorter than those of QDs alone, because the surface charge of QDs-Ab conjugates decreased after the carboxyl groups on the surface of QDs were coupled with amino groups on Abs. Driven by high pressure forward electroosmosis, QDs and QDs-Ab conjugates moved towards the negative electrode at different migration speeds, due to the relatively less negative charge on the surface of the QDs-Ab conjugates compared with QDs, QDs-Abs faced less repulsive force, faster migration speed and shorter time to reach the fluorescence detection window. The above results also demonstrated the coupling effectiveness of QDs.

The probe’s stability was an important affecting factor to its application. The QDs-Ab fluorescent probes were stored in the dark for 30 d, and their zeta potentials, hydrated particle sizes, and PDIs were analyzed every 5 d. The results showed that the zeta potentials of BCDs-KAb, GQDs-HAb, and RQDs-LAb remained basically unchanged (Fig. 4 A-C); at the same time, the hydrated particle sizes did not change significantly, indicating no obvious agglomeration phenomenon; PDI was maintained between 0.2 and 0.3 (Fig. 4D-F), showing that the particle size distribution was uniform and the dispersibility was good.

In fluorescence imaging, high intensity excitation will lead to photobleaching phenomenon, which limits the long-term observation of the labeled target. Therefore, the photobleaching resistance of the QDs-Ab fluorescent probes was explored. In the experiment, after the QDs-Ab fluorescent probes and Rh6G were continuously irradiated under ultraviolet light, their fluorescence spectra were collected at intervals. The results showed that the fluorescence of Rh6G was decayed sharply and quenched within 20 min basically; however, the fluorescence intensity of the QDs-Ab fluorescent probes did not change significantly and remained above 80 % (Fig. 4G-I), indicating that QDs-Ab probes had excellent fluorescence stability.

The structural integrity of the antibody protein in fluorescent probe determined whether it could effectively recognize the antigen, so the circular dichroism of Ab and QDs-Ab were analyzed. The results showed that the curves of Ab and QDs-Ab were basically consistent (Fig. 4J-L), indicating that the structure of Ab proteins were intact and not damaged after conjugation, and still maintained their structure, so they could be used for subsequent specific targeting imaging.

In order to label and imaging immune cells in the liver, the distribution of different cells in the liver was examined by H&E staining firstly. The results showed that the hepatic lobule was the landmark structure and basic structural unit of the liver (Fig. 5 ), and the cavity in the middle was the central vein (CV); the large and round blue purple was the nucleus of the liver parenchyma cells, and the hepatic parenchymal cells (HCs) were arranged radially around the center of the CV, which was called the liver plate; the hepatic sinusoids were formed between the liver plates, which were also arranged radially around CV; liver sinusoidal endothelial cells (LSECs), as the main cell group of hepatic non-parenchymal cells, were located on the hepatic sinusoid wall in the hepatic blood sinusoids, hepatic kupffer cells (KCs) were located in the hepatic sinusoids with phagocytic function, the arrangement was basically consistent with the hepatic sinuses, which were arranged radially, while hepatic stellate cells (HSCs) were hard to be observed in HE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM of mouse liver ultrathin section
ce:section.ce:para: In order to observe the distribution of non-parenchymal cells in the liver more directly, the liver was processed with ultrathin section and imaged by TEM. The results showed that LSECs were close to the hepatic sinusoidal wall (Fig. 6 ), and KCs were distributed in the hepatic sinusoidal cavity, which was consistent with liver H&E staining; perisinusoidal space was formed between LSECs and HCs, and HSCs were located in the perisinusoidal space.

Before multi-color fluorescence imaging of immune cells on liver frozen section, in order to locate the distribution of liver immune cells more accurately, immunofluorescence imaging of KCs, HSCs, and LSECs on liver frozen section was performed respectively. Firstly, at 37 °C, the section was incubated successively with anti-EMR1 as the primary antibody (it could specifically bind to the mouse EGF-like module-containing mucin-like hormone receptor-like 1 on the surface of macrophages), and the blue fluorescent dye AMCA labeled secondary antibody (AMCA-Ab), and the liver macrophages were immunofluorescence imaged as the standard. The results showed that blue fluorescence was radially arranged on the section (Fig. 7 ), and the collected fluorescence spectrum was consistent with that of AMCA, the emission peak was located at 462 nm, which was due to the fact that hepatic KCs were located in the hepatic sinusoids, so they were arranged radially, and it was consistent with the arrangement presented in liver H&E. At the same time, it was also found that the fluorescence of the control section directly incubated with the fluorescent secondary antibody without adding the primary antibody was obviously weaker, and the fluorescence intensity was also significantly lower than that of the experimental group treated with the primary antibody/secondary antibody, showing that this indirect immunofluorescence could well label targeted cells. Similarly, liver macrophages on liver frozen sections were labeled with anti-EMR1 and BCDs-KAb at −20 and 37 °C respectively, and observed by confocal microscopy. The results showed that the blue fluorescence from the BCDs-KAb probe was observed on both of frozen sections, and their distribution pattern was consistent with that of the standard group, in a radial arrangement, the fluorescence emission peak position (435 nm) was also consistent with that of BCDs-KAb probe, and the fluorescence of control section without primary antibody was not obvious. The above results indicated that macrophages on liver frozen section could be well specifically labeled and imaged with our prepared BCDs-KAb fluorescent probe below freezing point.

Similarly, HSCs on liver frozen section were immunofluorescence imaged using anti-GFAP as the primary antibody (it could specifically bind the glial fibrillary acidic protein on HSCs) and green fluorescent dye FITC-labeled secondary antibody (FITC-Ab) as standard, the results showed that FITC green fluorescence appeared on the liver frozen section (Fig. 8 ), showing scattered dot distribution, and its fluorescence emission peak corresponded to the fluorescence of FITC at around 523 nm, meanwhile, the fluorescence of the control section without primary antibody was obviously weaker. This distribution might be due to the fact that HSCs were located in the perisinusoidal space and relatively few in number, thus showing a sporadic punctate distribution on liver slice. Similarly, HSCs on the liver frozen sections were fluorescently imaged with anti-GFAP and GQDs-HAb at −20 and 37 °C, respectively. The results showed that punctate green fluorescence appeared on both of liver frozen sections, which was consistent with the results of the standard section, moreover, the fluorescence emission peaks on the liver frozen sections were located at about 543 nm, which were consistent with the emission peak of the GQDs-HAb fluorescent probe, indicating that the green fluorescence on liver frozen sections was derived from the GQDs-HAb fluorescence probe, which further demonstrated that GQDs-HAb could specifically target and label the HSCs on the liver frozen section below the freezing point.

Anti-CD31 was also used as the primary antibody (it could specifically bind platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecules on the surface of LSECs) and the red fluorescent dye Cy3 labeled Ab (Cy3-Ab) as the standard fluorescent secondary antibody to fluorescent imaging of LSECs on liver frozen section. The results showed that a ring-shaped red fluorescence appeared on the slice (Fig. 9 ), and its emission peak was located at 580 nm, which was consistent with the fluorescence emission peak position of Cy3, while the fluorescence of the control section without primary antibody was obviously weaker. Since the LSECs were distributed on the hepatic sinusoidal wall, it presents a ring-shaped structure of the hepatic sinusoidal wall. Meanwhile, the LSECs on frozen sections were immunofluorescence imaged with anti-CD31 as the primary antibody and RQDs-LAb as the fluorescence secondary antibody at −20 and 37 °C respectively, similar to the standard control section, circular red fluorescence also appeared on both of the liver frozen sections, and the fluorescence spectra were consistent with that of the RQDs-LAb probe, indicating that RQDs-LAb could successfully label the LSECs on the liver frozen section below the freezing point.

Observing the labeling effect of three types of cells, it could be also found that the fluorescence intensity of section at −20 °C was higher than that at 37 °C under the three conditions (Figs. 7-9), indicating that the performance of the fluorescent probe at low temperature was improved, so KCs, HSCs, and LSECs had better labeling effect below freezing point. At the same time, it could be also seen that, as negative controls, the fluorescence intensity of liver frozen sections incubated with only fluorescent secondary antibody without primary antibody was very weak, indicating that QDs-Ab fluorescent probes could specifically target liver immune cells at −20 °C.

Next, multicolor imaging of KCs, HSCs, and LSECs was performed on liver frozen section. In the experiment, we first added anti-GFAP primary antibody and FITC-Ab probe to liver frozen section, washed with PBS, then added anti-EMR1 and AMCA-Ab fluorescent secondary antibody successively, washed with PBS, and finally anti-CD31 primary antibody and Cy3-Ab fluorescence secondary antibody were added sequentially, and the section was used as standard for observation under an inverted fluorescence microscope after coverslip-sealing. The results showed that only the blue fluorescence in the radial arrangement was observed on the liver frozen section under 405 nm excitation, which was consistent with the results in Fig. 7, indicating hepatic macrophages; only punctate green fluorescence appeared on the slice under 488 nm excitation, which was consistent with the imaging results in Fig. 8, indicating that HSCs were successfully labeled; only ring-shaped red fluorescence appeared on the slice at the excitation of 561 nm, which was consistent with the imaging effect in Fig. 9. The collected fluorescence emission spectra were also consistent with the fluorescence spectra of FITC, AMCA, and Cy3, indicating that the fluorescence was indeed from the FITC-Ab, AMCA-Ab, and Cy3-Ab probes. At the same time, in order to exclude non-specific reaction, PBS was used to replace the three primary antibodies, and FITC-Ab, AMCA-Ab, and Cy3-Ab probes were added as the second antibodies in turn, it was found that it presented weak fluorescence and extremely low intensity on the liver frozen section. After the three channel results were merged, three different colors of fluorescence, blue, green, and red, could be observed on the liver frozen section, and their distribution and arrangement were consistent with the position distribution of liver H&E staining and ultrathin section TEM, indicating that KCs, HSCs, and LSECs were specifically labeled (Fig. 10 ), but could not be imaged at the same time.

Although organic fluorescent dyes could achieve multicolor imaging, however, they required multiple wavelengths of light to excite different fluorescent dyes, which limited their practical application. The same wavelength of light could excite different QDs to emit different colors of fluorescence, so it was particularly suitable for multi-signal source synchronous labeling and imaging. In immunofluorescence triple labeling, three fluorescent antibodies were added to the liver frozen section at the same time, although this was a simple and rapid method. However, when multiple antigens existed in the same tissue and the contents of various antigens differed greatly, the staining of the antigen with the highest concentration would prevent the staining of another antigens. In this case, the staining should be done separately, the lowest content antigen should be stained firstly, and then the higher content antigen in turn, and finally staining the antigen with the highest amount.

Among the hepatic immune cells, the content of hepatic stellate cell (HSC) was 10 %, and that of Kupffer cell (KC) was 30 %, and the content of hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cell (LSEC) was 60 %. Therefore, the order of anti-GFAP & GQDs-HAb, anti-EMR1 & BCDs-KAb, anti-CD31 & RQDs-LAb was selected to label the liver frozen section with fluorescence probes. Therefore, anti-GFAP and GQDs-HAb were added to liver frozen section, then anti-EMR1 and BCDs-KAb, respectively, and finally anti-CD31 and RQDs-LAb were added successively, liver frozen section was immunofluorescence imaged at −20 and 37 °C respectively, it was found that blue, green and red fluorescence simultaneously appeared on the liver frozen section under 405 nm excitation light (Figs. 11 and 12
), and their distribution and arrangement were consistent with those of the fluorescent dye probes as the reference standard, KCs with blue fluorescence in radial distribution, HSCs with green fluorescence in punctate distribution, and LSECs with annular red fluorescence, respectively. Moreover, three emission peaks appeared in the fluorescence spectrum at the same time, located at about 436 nm, 544 nm, and 609 nm respectively, which were close to the fluorescence emission peaks of BCDs-KAb, GQDs-HAb, and RQDs-LAb.

At the same time, if only GQDs-HAb, BCDs-KAb, and RQDs-LAb fluorescent secondary antibodies (QDs-Ab) were added separately without primary antibody as the control, no obvious fluorescence was observed on liver frozen section, and the fluorescence intensity was very weak. In addition, the native QDs (GQDs, BCDs, and RQDs) without Ab functionalization were also adopted as the control to imaging the liver immune cells on the liver frozen section at 37 and −20 °C respectively, and the results were consistent with those of only adding QDs-Ab fluorescent secondary antibody without primary antibody (Figs. 11 and 12), no obvious fluorescence appeared, therefore, the nonspecific binding of native QDs to liver immune cells was eliminated. The above results all indicated that the QD fluorescence probe could realize synchronous labeling and multi-color simultaneous imaging of various liver immune cells at the same excitation wavelength.

In this paper, carbon QDs, CdTe@CdS, and CdSe@ZnS QDs with blue, green, and red fluorescence were coupled with different antibodies, and KCl was used as antifreeze to establish a new method for simultaneous labeling and multicolor fluorescence imaging of KCs, HSCs and LSECs on mouse liver frozen section at − 20 °C below freezing point. This method provides important technical support for our in-depth understanding of the distribution of immune cells in the liver and the structure and function of liver.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

Yan-Fei He: Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Kai Cheng: Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zi-Tao Zhong: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision, Validation. Xiao-Lin Hou: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Chang-Zhi An: Data curation, Validation, Formal analysis. Wei Chen: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Bo Liu: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yuan-Di Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0700402), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81971658). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST), the Research Core Facilities for Life Science (HUST) and the Center for Nanoscale Characterization & Devices (CNCD) at WNLO of HUST for the help of measurement.
